title: Per-million price proposal and benchmarks
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@hearthlytics.com>, Hearthlytics Procurement <procurement@hearthlytics.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 29 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Per-million price proposal and benchmarks

Hi Liam / Hearthlytics team — thanks again for the call this morning. Quick written recap and next steps so we’re aligned: 

Summary of where Hearthlytics landed on requirements:
- Primary workload: high-volume generative prompts (completion tokens) for customer-facing features + occasional embedding workloads for semantic search.
- Expected monthly consumption: 12M–25M generated tokens in month 1, scaling to 100M tokens/month by Q4 if pilot goes well.
- Target latency SLO: p95 < 450ms for short prompts; tolerance for higher latency on long-form jobs.

Cost & pricing questions you asked us to qualify:
1) Target price range you’re hoping to land at (per 1M tokens).
2) Preferred model mix (open weights vs hosted proprietary).
3) Procurement rhythm and budget window (monthly vs committed quarterly).

Redwood preliminary guidance (ballpark ranges, will refine once we get exact model mix and sequence-length distribution):
- Hosted open-model variants (quantized LLaMA/Meta-style): $3–$8 per 1M tokens depending on quant/throughput profile and caching.
- Dedicated / committed capacity (reserved GPU pools + commitment): $1.8–$4.5 per 1M tokens for mature steady-state 100M+/mo volumes.
- Fallback / high-availability blends that route to higher-tier models (lower accuracy to lower-cost mix): +/- 15% on unit price to preserve latency SLOs.

Attachments: redwood_cost_model_v1.xlsx (simple unit-econ calculator) and benchmark_vs_hosts.pdf (public host comparisons vs OpenAI/Anthropic and selected open-hosts).

Next steps we propose: 
- You to confirm target $/1M range that would make the pilot green-lit internally. (Procurement preferred band + absolute maximum.)
- You to share a short sample of typical prompt length distribution (P50/P90 token lengths) and the % of requests that require embeddings vs completions.
- We will return an updated cost model (v2) with 3 scenarios: self-host mix, dedicated reserved, and hosted fallbacks — plus comparative line items vs OpenAI and Anthropic.

If it helps, we can schedule a 30-min procurement + infra deep-dive next week to align on commitment terms and SLA language.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0193


From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@hearthlytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Hearthlytics Procurement <procurement@hearthlytics.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 29 May 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Per-million price proposal and benchmarks

Thanks Ben — useful framing. A couple quick clarifications before we kick off procurement: 

1) For benchmarking we need explicit per-1M token comparisons vs OpenAI (gpt-4.1/4o) and Anthropic (Claude X) for both \"generation\" and \"embeddings\" where applicable. Can you call out the assumptions (tokenization parity, seq length) used for each host?
2) Our procurement committee is asking whether Redwood can get within $4.50/1M tokens for generation in a committed (12-month) contract at 100M/mo steady-state. Is that achievable with Dedicated or host-mix?
3) We have some security gating for embeddings — if you can confirm the isolation/privacy model for embedding operations (where vectors are stored and how long transient caches live), that will help approvals.

If you can reply with a short table (OpenAI / Anthropic / Redwood - $/1M generation - $/1M embeddings - notes/assumptions) that would speed things up. Also, add procurement copies to any redlines or contract language.

— Liam
Liam O'Connor
Head of Platform, Hearthlytics
liam.oconnor@hearthlytics.com


From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 29 May 2027 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: quick alignment on numbers (do not share until we update v2)

Ben — quick internal note after Liam's ask: 
- We can hit sub-$4.50/1M generation with a 12-month committed Dedicated deal at 100M/mo, assuming a 20% reserved GPU utilization uplift and acceptance of our quantized model variants for the bulk of inference. That maps to the lower end of the "Dedicated" range I sent earlier.
- For hosted (no commitment) parity vs OpenAI/Anthropic: we are generally +10–30% vs their public per-1M list price for similar accuracy tiers; variance driven by caching opportunities and request distribution.
- Embeddings pricing: depends on whether customer needs dedicated vector storage (private VPC KMS + HSM keys) — dedicated embeddings with private storage will be ~25–40% higher than multi-tenant embeddings.

Attachments to include in the outward message: redwood_cost_model_v2.xlsx (updated assumptions), and a short note on embedding isolation that legal can sign off on. I can draft the short comparison table Ben asked for — will need 30–45 min.

M.
Monica Patel
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
monica_patel@redwood.com


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@hearthlytics.com>, Hearthlytics Procurement <procurement@hearthlytics.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 29 May 2027 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Per-million price proposal and benchmarks

Liam — thanks for the clear asks. See the high-level table below (we'll send an attached spreadsheet with exact assumptions and seq-lengths):

Host / $/1M generation (est) / $/1M embeddings (est) / Notes
- OpenAI (gpt-4.1 family) / $8.00–$12.00 / $5.50–$9.00 / public list prices, higher for lower-latency and function-calling variants
- Anthropic (Claude X) / $7.00–$11.00 / $5.00–$8.50 / similar accuracy tiers; some regional pricing variance
- Redwood (hosted open variants) / $3.00–$8.00 / $3.50–$7.00 / depends on quantization & caching; lower for large committed reservations
- Redwood (Dedicated, 12mo, 100M/mo steady-state) / $1.80–$4.50 / $2.50–$4.00 / committed capacity pricing assumes 20% utilization uplift & reserved GPU pools

Answering your specific questions: 
- Can we get to $4.50/1M for generation on a committed 12mo at 100M/mo? Yes — within the Dedicated pricing band above, $4.50 is achievable at conservative utilization assumptions. We will show sensitivity to utilization and burst profiles in the spreadsheet.
- Benchmark assumptions: For the table above we used tokenization parity (BPE approx), a mixed P50/P90 sequence length of 50/400 for generation, and embeddings based on 768–2048 dimension encoders depending on host. Full calc in attachment.
- Embedding isolation: for Dedicated deployments we support private vector stores with customer KMS keys and no shared tenant indices. For hosted options we offer per-customer index isolation and can configure retention windows; details in the embedding isolation note in the v2 attachment.

Attachments: redwood_cost_model_v2.xlsx, benchmark_vs_hosts.pdf (updated). Next step: can we schedule a 30-min procurement + SRE call next Tue (Jun 1) 10am PT to lock commitment terms and SLA language? If that works, I'll send a calendar invite and a short procurement checklist (PO cadence, invoicing, required contract clauses).

Best —
Ben
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0193

title: Intro & demo request — traffic debrief and scheduling
preview:
From: Samira Khan <samira@brightcart.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 28 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Demo request after signup — BrightCart traffic snapshot attached

Hi Ben,

We signed up for Redwood's self-serve yesterday and I got a ping from our SDR outreach. I'm Samira (Head of ML at BrightCart). We want to get a short demo to validate performance and discuss a small pilot. I attached a quick traffic snapshot (traffic_profile.xlsx).

High level:
- Primary use case: customer support message summarization + semantic search over support docs (embeddings + reranking).
- Current footprint: ~20k daily active users, ~30k inference requests/day across web + mobile, average request ~250 tokens.
- Peak: ~25 RPS sustained bursts for 2–3 minutes during sales drops.
- Timeline: pilot ideally starting mid-July; production target in Q4 if POC looks good.

Two asks:
1) Can we book a 30-min demo next week to cover architecture and pilot sizing?
2) Can you confirm what information you need from our side ahead of the call (SLOs, security checklist, test credentials)?

Thanks — happy to loop in Daniel (ops) if needed.

Best,
Samira
Samira Khan
Head of ML, BrightCart AI
---
Attachment: traffic_profile.xlsx (spreadsheet, 48KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira@brightcart.ai>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 28 May 2027 09:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Demo request after signup — BrightCart traffic snapshot attached

Hi Samira — really appreciate the sign-up and the context. I'm Ben on the sales team; looping in Aditya (solutions eng) so he can prep sizing notes. Quick responses and next steps below.

Short answers / asks:
- Use case: great — summarization + retrieval is a common pattern for us. We'll cover embeddings vs. reranking tradeoffs in the demo.
- Peak and concurrency: thank you for the numbers; can you confirm the 25 RPS is for model inference (tokens out) or includes front-end aggregation? Also, do you expect many long-form responses or mostly short summaries?
- Data & security: are you planning to send PII to the API? Do you require VPC/private deployment or is hosted acceptable for the pilot?
- Timeline & decision maker: mid-July pilot sounds reasonable. Who on your side will sign off on pilot success?

Proposed slots (all PST):
- Tue Jun 1 — 10:00–10:30 AM
- Wed Jun 2 — 2:00–2:30 PM
- Thu Jun 3 — 11:30 AM–12:00 PM

Pick any slot or use my calendar: https://calendly.com/ben-carter/30min.

I'll attach a short quickstart PDF and Aditya can add a pre-demo checklist (sample SLOs, suggested test prompt set).

Cheers,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference
---
Attachment: redwood-quickstart.pdf (pdf, 120KB)

From: Samira Khan <samira@brightcart.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Daniel Ortiz <daniel@brightcart.ai>
Date: Sat, 29 May 2027 10:05:00 -07:00

title: Request: Short-term quota regrade + launch-week discount structure
preview:
From: Nathan Cole <nathan.cole@summitcart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@summitcart.com>
Date: Thu, 27 May 2027 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Quick help — launch week capacity + pricing question

Ben —

We’re ramping to production for our May 2027 campaign and the traffic forecast attached shows a sharp burst for the week of June 6–12. The baseline commit we have today will not cover the spike (estimate ~4–5x normal QPS).

Two questions:
1) Can Redwood provision a temporary quota bump / warm pool for that 7-day window? We need something that doesn’t require a long-term commit but gives guaranteed throughput.
2) If you can, what pricing options do you have for short windows like this? We’re looking for an all-in launch-week package (flat fee + capped overrun) or an uplift with a small committed discount.

We have procurement on copy; they’ll need the PO and amendment language for approvals. Attached: launch-week-traffic-forecast.xlsx.

Thanks —
Nathan Cole
Head of Engineering, SummitCart
+nathan.cole@summitcart.com

---
(Attachment: launch-week-traffic-forecast.xlsx)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Nathan Cole <nathan.cole@summitcart.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 27 May 2027 16:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick help — launch week capacity + pricing question

Nathan —

Thanks for the forecast, that helps a lot. Short answer: yes, we can support a short-window uplift with a couple of guardrail approaches depending on how you want to trade latency vs cost predictability. High-level options: 

- Ephemeral headroom (recommended): temporary quota regrade / warm pool for the defined window. No long-term commit; we reserve capacity for the week. Pricing: a one-week flat reservation fee + usage at your current pay-as-you-go rate for overrun. We can cap overrun exposure with a fixed cap and soft throttles.

- Temporary tier bump with a launch discount: convert the week to a higher throughput tier and apply a one-time discount (we typically offer a stepped discount tied to volume bands). This is simpler billing-wise but requires a short amendment.

- Hybrid: small prepaid credit that covers the first X tokens, then discounted overage pricing afterwards. This is often what customers prefer for predictable launches.

I’ve attached a draft provisional quote and a sample amendment template that procurement can review. From an operational perspective, we’ll need:
- Final confirmed window (start/end times)
- Expected peak qps and acceptable latency SLOs
- PO or purchasing contact + preferred billing terms

Monica / Karthik — looping you in to confirm a recommended discount cadence + any precedents for short-window true-ups (deal SC-2027-045).

Nathan — if you want, I can put together a concrete quote for the hybrid structure (flat reservation + token buffer + capped overrun) and a short timeline for activation (we can typically flip an ephemeral headroom in 48–72 hours once the amendment is signed).

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0199

---
(Attachment: provisional-quote-SummitCart.pdf)
(Attachment: amendment-draft-SC.docx)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 28 May 2027 09:07:00 -0700
Subject: INTERNAL: suggested pricing for SummitCart launch-week

Ben / Monica —

On the SummitCart uplift: I recommend we offer the hybrid structure Ben proposed with a short-window concession to close this quickly. My suggested commercial terms: 

- Reservation fee: $6,500 for the 7-day reserved warm pool (covers dedicated headroom up to projected peak).
- Prepaid token buffer: $12,000 credit that applies to the first X tokens consumed during the window (we will map X once we confirm the forecast scaling).
- Discounted overrun: 30% off standard PAYG rate for any consumption beyond the prepaid buffer for that week.
- True-up: one-time invoice true-up at the end of the month; if final usage < prepaid buffer we refund the difference pro rata.
- Activation SLA: we can have the warm pool live within 72 hours of signed amendment + PO.

Notes: this is a short-window promotional concession (deal_id SC-2027-045). If SummitCart wants to convert to a committed tier after the launch, we can grandfather a portion of the discount into the first quarter commit.

If you want, I can run the numbers vs cost for us and propose a final margin-protecting floor.

Karthik
Head of Deals, Redwood Inference

---
(Internal only)

Cc: Procurement <procurement@summitcart.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 29 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick help — launch week capacity + pricing question

Thanks, Ben. This looks workable. Procurement has reviewed the amendment draft and asked for the PO timeline — they can get paperwork to you by Tuesday, June 1 if we lock the numbers today. Procurement contact: Laura Hsu <laura.hsu@summitcart.com>.

We accept the hybrid structure you recommended. Please proceed with the provisional reservation + prepaid buffer as Karthik outlined (reservation fee $6,500 + $12,000 prepaid buffer, 30% overrun discount). Once the PO is raised we expect activation within the 72-hour SLA.

Quick confirmations needed for your ops team:
- Activation window start: 2027-06-06T00:00:00Z
- End: 2027-06-12T23:59:59Z
- Target latency SLO: p95 < 220ms (token generation end-to-end)

I’ll have Laura send PO and signed amendment this week. Appreciate the quick turnaround.

Cheers,

title: Authorization request: temporary uptime floor & latency safeguard for QuantumBridge pilot
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-20T09:13:00-07:00
Subject: Authorization request: temporary uptime floor & latency safeguard for QuantumBridge pilot

Team —

Quick summary: QuantumBridge (pilot, expected TCV $420k over 12mo) is asking for two temporary concessions to get the POC started this quarter:

- Uptime floor: allow 99.5% for the first 60 days of the pilot (they requested 99.0; deal desk recommends 99.5).
- Latency SLO: accept 95th percentile latency of 700ms for the first 30 days with an agreed mitigation plan; service credit structure capped to 25% of pilot TCV or $50,000 (whichever is lower).

Commercial concession: Sales is proposing a 20% pilot discount in exchange for an accelerated 60‑day feedback loop and a post‑pilot SLA full remediation commitment.

Attachments: deal_summary_QBR-042.xlsx, quantumbridge_mop_v2.pdf, qbridge_security_snapshot.pdf.

Why I need signoff: the uptime floor and the credit cap are outside standard terms. Deal Desk (Ben) flagged this as an exec‑level approval given precedent risk and the uplift to onboarding support costs. If we approve, Legal will prepare a short amendment and Finance will route invoicing expectations.

Ask: Jonas — can you provide an authorization (or suggested edits) on the uptime floor + credit cap? If you’re OK, Ben will coordinate with Legal and I’ll confirm timelines with QuantumBridge.

Thanks —
Avery
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
avery_johnson@redwood.ai
[attachment: deal_summary_QBR-042.xlsx]
[attachment: quantumbridge_mop_v2.pdf]


From: Samir Patel <samir@quantumbridge.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elaine Wu <elaine.wu@quantumbridge.com>
Date: 2027-05-21T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Authorization request: temporary uptime floor & latency safeguard for QuantumBridge pilot

Avery / Marissa —

Thanks for the clear summary. We’re on a tight schedule for our October launch pilot and the legal team asked that we confirm concessions by end of this week so internal procurement can sign the PO. A couple points from our side:

- 99.5% uptime for 60 days is workable as long as there’s a clear rollback and escalation path to dedicated capacity if we hit sustained incidents.
- For latency, 95th percentile at 700ms is acceptable during initial testing as long as we get telemetry access to the same dashboards you use and a weekly sync.

We’ve attached a one‑page acceptance form with the dates we need. Happy to hop on a short call if that helps move things faster.

—Samir
Samir Patel
Head of Platform, QuantumBridge
samir@quantumbridge.com
[attachment: qbridge_acceptance_timeline.pdf]


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-24T10:15:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Authorization request: temporary uptime floor & latency safeguard for QuantumBridge pilot

Folks —

Deal Desk review (short):

- Business impact: Pilot is strategically valuable (financial services vertical, strong reference potential). Risk of precedent is medium.
- Proposed guardrails (my recommendation for approval):
  * Uptime floor: 99.5% for first 60 days only; non‑recurring remedy (one time) if breached: service credit up to 10% of pilot invoices for a single incident, aggregated cap at 25% of pilot TCV or $50k cap.
  * Latency SLO: 95th ≤ 700ms for first 30 days. If not met for more than two non‑consecutive weeks, automatic remediation plan (2 weeks of dedicated optimization support) + incremental credits limited to 15% of pilot invoices.
  * Monitoring & access: Grant QuantumBridge read‑only access to our runtime dashboards and agree to weekly sync for the pilot duration.
  * Pricing trade: 20% pilot discount + one‑time onboarding support hours (estimated 40 hrs) included.

Legal note: we received a redline (qbridge_term_redline.docx). Key asks from Legal: tighten the definition of \"downtime\" to exclude scheduled maintenance with 48‑hour notice; require root‑cause report within 10 business days for any incident claimed.

If execs sign off, I’ll route the exact amendment to Legal to prepare a DocuSign and confirm the credit calculation language.

Recommendation: Approve with the guardrails above. Jonas — can you sign off on the authority to accept temporary SLO relaxations and the credit cap?

Ben Carter
Senior Deal Desk Analyst, Redwood Inference
[attachment: qbridge_term_redline.docx]


From: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-28T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Authorization request: temporary uptime floor & latency safeguard for QuantumBridge pilot

Team —

Thanks for the clear framing and for surfacing the Legal redlines. I approve the Deal Desk recommendation with the following explicit conditions (recording here so Legal/Finance have a single source of truth):

1) Uptime concession: 99.5% for 60 days only; remedy language limited to the caps Ben proposed (25% of pilot TCV / $50k). No ongoing SLO change beyond the pilot period.
2) Latency: 95th ≤ 700ms for first 30 days, with the two‑week remediation path if thresholds are missed.
3) Quantified trade: 20% pilot discount as proposed and inclusion of 40 onboarding hours.
4) Monitoring & rollback: QuantumBridge gets read‑only dashboard access; Redwood reserves the right to migrate them to a higher‑capacity pod (at our discretion) if repeated incidents occur — migration costs are scoped to Redwood unless caused by customer misuse.
5) Legal: incorporate wording that scheduled maintenance is excluded if customers receive 48‑hour notice; require RCA within 10 business days.

Ben — proceed to finalize the amendment with Legal and send the DocuSign to Samir and Elaine. Avery/Marissa — confirm final invoice schedule and confirm the pilot start date once the signed amendment is back.

Jonas Weber
VP Revenue Operations, Redwood Inference
[Signed electronically]

  * Uptime floor: 99.5% for first 60 days only; non‑recurring remedy (one time) if breached: service credit up to 10% of pilot invoices for a single incident, aggregated cap at 25% of pilot TCV or $50k cap.
  * Latency SLO: 95th ≤ 700ms for first 30 days. If not met for more than two non‑consecutive weeks, automatic remediation plan (2 weeks of dedicated optimization support) + incremental credits limited to 15% of pilot invoices.
  * Monitoring & access: Grant QuantumBridge read‑only access to our runtime dashboards and agree to weekly sync for the pilot duration.
  * Pricing trade: 20% pilot discount + one‑time onboarding support hours (estimated 40 hrs) included.

Legal note: we received a redline (qbridge_term_redline.docx). Key asks from Legal: tighten the definition of \"downtime\" to exclude scheduled maintenance with 48‑hour notice; require root‑cause report within 10 business days for any incident claimed.

If execs sign off, I’ll route the exact amendment to Legal to prepare a DocuSign and confirm the credit calculation language.

Recommendation: Approve with the guardrails above. Jonas — can you sign off on the authority to accept temporary SLO relaxations and the credit cap?

Ben Carter
Senior Deal Desk Analyst, Redwood Inference
[attachment: qbridge_term_redline.docx]


From: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-28T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Authorization request: temporary uptime floor & latency safeguard for QuantumBridge pilot

Team —

Thanks for the clear framing and for surfacing the Legal redlines. I approve the Deal Desk recommendation with the following explicit conditions (recording here so Legal/Finance have a single source of truth):

1) Uptime concession: 99.5% for 60 days only; remedy language limited to the caps Ben proposed (25% of pilot TCV / $50k). No ongoing SLO change beyond the pilot period.
2) Latency: 95th ≤ 700ms for first 30 days, with the two‑week remediation path if thresholds are missed.
3) Quantified trade: 20% pilot discount as proposed and inclusion of 40 onboarding hours.
4) Monitoring & rollback: QuantumBridge gets read‑only dashboard access; Redwood reserves the right to migrate them to a higher‑capacity pod (at our discretion) if repeated incidents occur — migration costs are scoped to Redwood unless caused by customer misuse.
5) Legal: incorporate wording that scheduled maintenance is excluded if customers receive 48‑hour notice; require RCA within 10 business days.

Ben — proceed to finalize the amendment with Legal and send the DocuSign to Samir and Elaine. Avery/Marissa — confirm final invoice schedule and confirm the pilot start date once the signed amendment is back.

Jonas Weber
VP Revenue Operations, Redwood Inference

title: Gradient commit curve proposal for Hypersynth - finalizing ramp and term tradeoffs
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Singh <ava@hypersynth.ai>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, May 14, 2027 at 10:04 AM PDT
Subject: Gradient commit curve proposal for Hypersynth - initial model

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the time today. Attached is the Excel model I promised — it lays out a 'gradient commit curve' where Hypersynth ramps GPU and TPM commitments over 18 months rather than a single front-loaded tranche. Key highlights:

- Year 1: 40% of committed GPUs / TPM as an annual prepay with 8% term discount
- Year 2: additional 30% (paid at start of year) -> incremental discount step to 10%
- Year 3: final 30% with 12% total term discount on the aggregate commit if benchmarks (throughput/latency) are met

We also show two failure modes in tab 3: underutilization (where you pay for spare capacity) and acceleration credits (if you accelerate ramp, a credit is applied to the subsequent year's prepay).

I've called out three optional protections for you: milestone crediting, an annual reconciliation with a 6-month lookback for smoothing, and an exit carve-out for the first 90 days of year 1 (protects you if workload changes materially).

Please review the spreadsheet (Hypersynth_Ramp_Model.xlsx) and let me know which pieces you want us to stretch tighter (bigger year-1) or looser (longer tail). Happy to jump on a quick call to walk through numbers.

Best,
Ben
Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference

-----Original Message-----

From: Ava Singh <ava@hypersynth.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Hypersynth Legal <legal@hypersynth.ai>
Date: Sat, May 15, 2027 at 09:12 AM PDT
Subject: Re: Gradient commit curve proposal for Hypersynth - initial model

Hi Ben,

Thanks — got the workbook. A few quick reactions after a first pass:

- We like the idea of a gradual ramp, but we need better downside protection if our production peaks shift into Q2 of Y2. Can you describe how the milestone crediting would work operationally?
- The 8%/10%/12% band looks promising. Can you confirm pricing parity with your standard Dedicated list for the equivalent capacity (GPU/hr and TPM)? We may ask for a floor guarantee.
- Legal will want a clear definition of what 'benchmarks' means for the Y3 uplift.

Can you also produce a sample SOW that maps to the three-year ramp (scope + acceptance criteria)? If you can send a version we can redline, that speeds things up.

—Ava
Head of Platform Ops
Hypersynth

On Mon, May 17, 2027 at 08:46 AM PDT Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai> wrote:

Ava —

Quick answers inline:

- Milestone crediting: If utilization is < 70% of the committed baseline for any calendar quarter, Hypersynth accrues a credit equal to 30% of the unused spend for that quarter. Credits are applied against future prepay invoices or as a one-time account credit on annual reconciliation. We show both options in the workbook.

- Pricing: The per-GPU and per-TPM rates in the model match our standard Dedicated list; the term discounts are applied on top of that list price. I'll include a short table in the SOW mapping list price -> discounted price for each year.

- Benchmarks definition: For the Y3 additional discount to vest, we require passing two SLOs in two consecutive months: 1) < 120 ms 95th token latency on the representative workload, and 2) >= 98% successful request rate (no OOM/worker crash) during the test window. We'll be explicit in the SOW.

Attached: Redwood_Commit_Terms.docx (terms summary), updated Hypersynth_Ramp_Model.xlsx (v2).

Propose a 30-min call Thursday to walk the SOW and the reconciliation mechanics.

Ben

-----
From: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Ava Singh <ava@hypersynth.ai>
Date: Thu, May 20, 2027 at 09:02 AM PDT
Subject: Re: Gradient commit curve proposal for Hypersynth - internal note

Ben — looping in a short internal note: if Hypersynth insists on front-loaded price floors, we can protect revenue by tightening the decommit window (90-day notice for year 2 reductions) and pushing a slightly higher early-termination charge for capacity reductions beyond 20%. I think a compromise on annual reconciliation + mild acceleration credits will get them comfortable.

Happy to join the Thursday call if needed.

—Lauren
VP, Sales

-----Original Message-----

From: Ava Singh <ava@hypersynth.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
Cc: Hypersynth Legal <legal@hypersynth.ai>
Date: Tue, May 25, 2027 at 11:18 AM PDT
Subject: Re: Gradient commit curve proposal for Hypersynth - follow up

Ben / Lauren —

Thanks for the clarifications. After our internal review, Hypersynth's procurement team proposed a modified curve:

- Year 1: 30% prepay instead of 40% (we have a tight hiring phase)
- Year 2: 40% prepay (push more weight into Y2)
- Year 3: 30% (same)

They are also asking that milestone credits be capped at 20% of annual prepay and that unused credits can be refunded if not applied within 12 months.

Legal flagged the Y3 "benchmark" language and asked for an objective test harness we can both run (shared repo + traffic replay instructions). Can you commit to providing a test harness for validation?

If you can accept the 30/40/30 split with the 20% cap on credits and the refund clause, we can move to redline the SOW this week.

—Ava

On Fri, May 28, 2027 at 04:22 PM PDT Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai> wrote:

Ava / Ben / Lauren —

Thanks for the back-and-forth. Ben and I reviewed Hypersynth's modified 30/40/30 split and here's our working counter that preserves Redwood's revenue profile while addressing Hypersynth's risk concerns:

1) Accept 30/40/30 capacity split.
2) Cap milestone credits at 20% of annual prepay and allow refund if unused within 12 months (agreed).
3) Provide a joint test harness repo and a traffic-replay spec for the Y3 benchmark validation; Redwood will run the first validation and share logs.
4) Add a reconciliation window that allows Hypersynth to shift up to +/-10% of the next year's commitment at the annual reconciliation at no penalty (gives you flexibility).

Attached: Hypersynth_SOW_v3.pdf (maps commitments -> acceptance criteria), updated Hypersynth_Ramp_Model.xlsx (v3), and a redline of Redwood_Commit_Terms.docx.

If this reflects what you need, we'll prepare a DocuSign envelope for signature and loop in finance for invoicing.

Thanks,
Monica Patel
Director, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

-----
From: Ava Singh <ava@hypersynth.ai>
To: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
Cc: Hypersynth Legal <legal@hypersynth.ai>
Date: Fri, May 28, 2027 at 05:10 PM PDT
Subject: Re: Gradient commit curve proposal for Hypersynth - finalizing ramp and term tradeoffs

Monica —

This is great. We appreciate the flexibility on the reconciliation carve and the commitment to a shared test harness. Legal will redline the SOW but the commercial terms look acceptable. Please go ahead and prepare the DocuSign and HubSpot deal update.

A few small asks before signature:
- Add an explicit definition of "representative workload" in the SOW (we'll include a snippet in the redline)
- Confirm invoice cadence for the year-1 30% prepay (we prefer monthly amortization vs a single upfront invoice).

If those are OK, we expect to route for signature next week.

—Ava

[Attachments referenced: Hypersynth_Ramp_Model.xlsx (v3), Hypersynth_SOW_v3.pdf, Redwood_Commit_Terms.docx]

Subject: Orion Analytics — procurement onboarding & privacy checklist

Hi Elena —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. As discussed, I'm kicking off the procurement onboarding thread so we can get you set up in our systems and confirm privacy requirements before we finalize the PO. To keep things moving, could you please confirm the following items by end of next week: 

1) Do you require Redwood to sign your DPA, or will you accept our standard DPA (attached) with a few redlines?
2) Please provide a current subprocessor list (or a pointer to where you host it).
3) Typical data retention needs for the integration (e.g., logs, telemetry, inference request bodies) and any minimum/maximum retention windows you expect.
4) PO billing contact, legal contact for contract review, and expected PO number format.

I've attached our standard DPA template and a short procurement onboarding form — filling the form will speed up the finance/PO process. CC'ing Marissa from procurement and Laura from legal for any questions.

Next steps: if it's helpful, we can do a 30-minute call next week to walk through the DPA and onboarding form.

Best,
Ben Carter
Sr. Account Executive
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123

Hi Ben —

Thanks for sending — appreciate the clear checklist. Quick answers below; I'll follow up with documents: 

1) DPA: We typically ask vendors to sign our DPA, but we can review your template first. We have a handful of commercial terms we prefer to propose (audit rights window, subprocessor notice timelines).

3) Retention: For telemetry we ask for a 90-day retention default, with ability to purge on request. For inference request bodies we require ephemeral handling (no storage beyond immediate processing) unless explicitly approved — we'll flag if the pilot requires storing inputs.
4) PO: Billing contact is accounts-payable@orionanalytics.com. Legal contact is contracts@orionanalytics.com. PO numbers are numeric, prefixed with ORN- (e.g., ORN-202705).

We can do a 30-minute sync on Wednesday 26th at 11am PT — does that work?

title: Cohort top-up proration & partner credit ask
preview:
From: Ethan Brooks <ethan@catalystlabs.org>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: ops@catalystlabs.org
Date: Sun, 23 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit

Hi Ben —

Quick ask from our side as the current cohort nears week 5: usage spiked (attaching snapshot) and we’re about to blow through the free cohort allowance earlier than expected. Two asks: 1) can Redwood prorate/bridge a one-time top-up of credits for this cohort to cover the overage through end of the cohort (roughly +$6k in spend), and 2) is there any partner credit you can apply (we co-run the program with LaunchFoundry and they typically get a small partner offset)?

We’re finalizing a follow-up SOW for the next cohort and would commit to a 6-month consumption minimum if we get a one-time accommodation here. Attaching usage snapshot + SOW draft. Happy to jump on a quick call today if helpful.

Thanks —
Ethan
Ops, Catalyst Labs
Attachments: cohort-usage-snapshot-May2027.csv (CSV), SOW-draft-v2.pdf (PDF)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Brooks <ethan@catalystlabs.org>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 23 May 2027 10:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit

Hi Ethan —

Thanks for the heads up and for the context. I pulled your usage and see the spike tied to overnight ingest jobs the cohort ran. Two quick notes: we typically handle small overages via credits tied to the program, and partner offsets are possible but handled as a separate partner-credit ledger.

I'll escalate internally for a one-time top-up to cover ~6k of the overage through the end of the cohort. Can you confirm the cohort end date (you have it as 2027-06-07 in the SOW draft) and whether the 6k is net of any credit card/transaction fees?

If you can confirm, I’ll chase a fast-track approval and propose a top-up schedule.

Best,
Ben
Sales, Redwood Inference
Cell: +1 (415) 555-0143


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 08:17:00 -0700
Subject: FW: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit — approval needed

Marissa / Naomi / Marcus —

Looping you in: Catalyst Labs (external) is asking for a one-time +$6k top-up to bridge an overage through 2027-06-07. They’re willing to commit to a 6-month minimum for the next cohort if we accommodate this.

What I’m proposing:
- Approve a one-time consumption credit of $4k from our program pool (accelerator allowance)
- Allow a partner-managed offset up to $2k if Marcus confirms partner ledger coverage (partner credit, not Redwood cash)
- Require the 6-month minimum on the follow-on SOW and a signed amendment to lock it in

This would keep the net Redwood impact at $4k while protecting us via contract. Any objections? If approved I’ll confirm to Ethan and send a DocuSign amendment template.

Cheers,
Ben


From: Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: FW: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit — approval needed

Ben / team —

I’m okay to approve the $4k one-time program credit on two conditions:
1) We get a signed amendment (simple one‑page) that adds a 6‑month minimum commitment with monthly reporting cadence.
2) The partner credit must be drawn from Marcus’s ledger and formally recorded (I need the partner credit memo).

If Marcus confirms the $2k partner offset and attaches the partner credit memo, finance will process the $4k credit and mark it as a non-refundable program allowance. Please route the DocuSign to naomi@redwood.com for signature.

Naomi
Head of Revenue Ops, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Brooks <ethan@catalystlabs.org>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 28 May 2027 15:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit

Hi Ethan —

Good news: we can do the bridge as discussed. Summary of what’s approved:

- Redwood will apply a one-time program credit of $4,000 against the cohort overage.
- We’ve asked our partner (LaunchFoundry) to confirm a partner offset up to $2,000; Marcus (cc’d) will confirm and send a partner credit memo.
- Condition: we need a signed one-page amendment to the current SOW committing to a 6‑month minimum and monthly usage check-ins. I’ve attached a proposed amendment and a top-up schedule for transparency.

Next steps for you:
1) Confirm cohort end date (we have 2027-06-07 in the SOW).
2) Sign the amendment (DocuSign link will follow once Marcus confirms partner memo).
3) Once signed, finance will apply the $4k credit within 3 business days.

If you’d like to discuss the partner offset mechanics, Marcus is on the thread and can explain what we need for the partner ledger.

Thanks — and appreciate the quick turnaround on the SOW amendment so we can avoid any interruption to your cohort.

Best,
Ben Carter
Redwood Inference — Sales

Subject: QBR prep: exec asks, roadmap exceptions & cross-func follow-ups

Team — thanks for a productive weekly sync. Recap and explicit owners below so we have a clean QBR playbook by next week. Please confirm owners/ETAs by EOD Friday.

1) Exec asks collection (Ben / Laura) — Ben: pull together the four high-priority asks that need exec sign-off for QBR. Deliverable: one-page summary with one-line impact and ask. ETA: 2027-06-01.

2) Exception register follow-ups (Marissa) — Marissa: review the open exceptions from roadmap (latency > target for short sequences; Dedicated capacity autoscale gaps). Action: add root cause and mitigation note in exception_register.csv and tag owner. ETA: 2027-05-31.

3) Model rollout compatibility (Karthik) — Identify top 2 kernel/quantization risks that could force fallback during canary. Action: propose fallback pairing and timeline with Research. ETA: 2027-05-30.

4) Production guardrails (Vivek) — Confirm SLO rollback thresholds and canary window for the QBR demo traffic pattern. Deliverable: Runbook stub + brief. ETA: 2027-05-29.

Notes / risks:
- Customer (Acme Analytics) wants a canary demo the week after QBR; we must avoid last-minute rollouts.
- We may need a short SLA exception for Dedicated during the demo traffic spike — Laura please advise.

Attachments: QBR_prompt_set_v2.xlsx (prompt/eval set); exception_register.csv (tracker)

Thanks — please keep replies inline where you update an ETA.

Ben Carter
Sales Director | Redwood Inference

title: Throughput anchoring & eviction tolerance — dedicated pool amendment
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Peter Liu <peter.liu@acelera.com>, Sara Kim <sara@acelera.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 23 May 2027 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Throughput anchoring & eviction tolerance — dedicated pool amendment

Hi Peter / Sara,

Thanks again for the deep dive yesterday. Attached is a short proposal (redwood_sla_proposal_v2.pdf) that sketches a potential amendment to the Dedicated capacity terms we discussed. High level:

- Anchor throughput: guarantee 85% of reserved GPU baseline for sustained windows (15m rolling).
- Eviction tolerance: implement a soft-eviction window (up to 90s) before aggressive preemption, with priority lanes for customer-specified critical traffic.
- Measurement & credits: p95 latency measured at route level; credits if p95 breaches during an anchored window.

We want a pragmatic addendum that protects Acelera's production latency without requiring a dedicated physical carve-out. Curious whether this framing addresses your procurement concerns and what additional guardrails you need (metrics, sample load profile, legal language).

If helpful, I can put a short pilot amendment together for a 30-day trial.

Thanks,
Ben
--
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123
Redwood Inference


From: Peter Liu <peter.liu@acelera.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Sara Kim <sara@acelera.com>
Cc: contracts@acelera.com
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 09:03:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Throughput anchoring & eviction tolerance — dedicated pool amendment

Ben — appreciate the clear summary and the PDF. A few immediate items from procurement and infra: 

1) Eviction tolerance: we cannot accept windows where critical requests see >30s preemption; our SLAs are tied to user-facing p95 < 300ms. A soft-eviction of 90s is a non-starter unless paired with guaranteed priority lanes for tagged traffic.

2) Anchoring at 85% of reserved baseline — that framing helps, but we need a deterministic mapping from our committed vGPU count to an absolute throughput floor (tokens/sec equivalence) so our billing projections match.

3) Measurement: we can share a sample workload (attached acelera_workload_sample.csv) and ask Redwood to run a synthetic against a staging pool to demonstrate behavior.

We'd be open to a 30-day pilot if the amendment includes: a) a defined experiment plan, b) hard limits on eviction for priority tags, and c) a credit formula if p95 exceeds our thresholds during the anchored windows. Can you loop in technical ops and legal?

Thanks,
Peter Liu
Head of Platform, Acelera Systems


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Peter Liu <peter.liu@acelera.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 11:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Throughput anchoring & eviction tolerance — dedicated pool amendment

Quick internal note and ask for next steps — forwarding Peter's reply. Peter, thanks for the specificity.

Evelyn / Naomi / Karthik: please see Peter's asks (attached sample workload).

- Legal: Peter wants a credit formula for p95 breaches during anchored windows. Naomi, can you draft a short amendment template that leaves the metric hooks flexible but includes a 30-day pilot termination/rollback?
- Product/Infra (Karthik): Acelera needs an absolute throughput mapping and a priority-tag lane with low eviction tolerance. Is it feasible to provide a soft-anchored floor expressed in tokens/sec per reserved vGPU and allocate a dedicated priority lane for tagged requests? Also, what observability hooks would we expose (route-level p95, sample rate)?

Goal: get a technical note + amendment skeleton this week so we can share a pilot DocuSign.

Ben
--
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Throughput anchoring & eviction tolerance — dedicated pool amendment

TL;DR: feasible as a pilot with conservative defaults and explicit telemetry. Technical proposal below — happy to tweak after a short sync.

Proposal (pilot shape):
- Throughput anchoring: map reserved vGPU -> baseline tokens/sec using our internal perf table. For example, 1xA100-equivalent reserved = ~1,200 tokens/sec sustained at 512 context. Anchor at 85% of that by default; we can publish the mapping in the amendment appendix.

- Priority lanes: implement labeled routing (customer-tag header). Priority lane will be carved from the same pool but served with preemption protection: during anchored windows, requests with the priority tag will be subject to a shorter eviction window (max 10s) and will be scheduled ahead of best-effort traffic.

- Eviction tolerance: make the default soft-eviction window 30s (not 90s) — consistent with Acelera's ask. If the pilot shows low disruption we can consider tightening.

- Credits & measurement: measure p95 at the application route (route alias), aggregated over 15m anchored windows. Credit formula suggestion: for each anchored window where p95 > contractual threshold, apply a prorated credit equal to 0.5% of monthly committed fee per occurrence, capped at 10% per billing period. Naomi, legal language will need to be precise on measurement signals and exclusions (maintenance, customer misconfig).

- Observability: expose route-level p95, sample traces on failure, and a daily headroom report during pilot. We'll run synthetic tests using Acelera's workload sample in staging first.

I'll draft the technical appendix and a one-page pilot plan today and share.

-Karthik
Principal Engineer, Serving Runtime


From: Peter Liu <peter.liu@acelera.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Sara Kim <sara@acelera.com>
Cc: contracts@acelera.com, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 27 May 2027 15:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Throughput anchoring & eviction tolerance — dedicated pool amendment

Ben / Karthik — this looks reasonable and aligned with what we discussed. Two small clarifications before we sign a pilot: 

1) The tokens/sec mapping for our committed vGPU sizes — please include the 512 and 1024 context rows in the appendix so we can reconcile with our forecasts.
2) The credit formula: we agree to the 0.5% per occurrence and 10% cap, but need an explicit maintenance exclusion window and a mechanism to request an audit of measurement signals (we'll ping your ops with timestamps if we disagree).

If you can add those two clarifications to the amendment, we're happy to proceed with the 30-day pilot. I've asked contracts to standby. Once we have the draft, send a DocuSign link and we'll execute.

Thanks — looking forward to the pilot.

Peter
--
Peter Liu
Head of Platform, Acelera Systems

Subject: New vendor onboarding + early-pay ask

Hi Avery,

Thanks for taking our call yesterday — really excited to get Silverpine set up as a Redwood supplier. We can start as soon as procurement has the vendor profile and PO routing. Two quick asks to help our cash cadence: 1) can we be onboarded onto Net45, and 2) would Redwood consider an early payment option (2% if paid within 10 days) for the first two invoices while we ramp? That would make a big difference during our initial implementation phase.

I've attached our completed vendor intake form and W-9. If you need anything else (banking, vendor code format, or certificates) let me know.

Best,
Lena Park

Silverpine Analytics

Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-24T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: New vendor onboarding + early-pay ask

Thanks Lena — appreciated and welcome aboard. Quick notes from procurement side:

- We got the intake form and W-9 (thanks).
- Standard onboarding timeline is ~3 business days once vendor info is validated and bank details are confirmed.
- Our default payable term for new suppliers is Net45, so that aligns with your request.
- Early-pay discounts are handled by Finance as a short pilot and require a signed supplemental payment agreement plus PO-level flags so our AP gateway routes invoices to the early-pay workflow.

Grace — can you confirm the required vendor portal fields and whether we need the Silverpine DBA or additional tax docs? Also tagging Ben from Finance to confirm feasibility of 2%/10 for an initial two-invoice pilot.

Action items for Silverpine: please share bank account (ACH) details and the remit email used by your billing system. We'll create the vendor record, generate the vendor code, and initiate a DocuSign supplemental for early-pay if Finance approves.

Avery Johnson
Head of Strategic Partnerships, Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwood.com

- Bank: Commerce Bank, Routing: 021000021, Account: ****6789 (Silverpine Analytics)
- Remit email: ap+invoices@silverpine.io

We are happy to sign any supplemental that formalizes 2% if paid within 10 days for the first two invoices. If Finance prefers a smaller fee, we're open to that too — main priority is quicker cash during the integration month.

Lena


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-26T09:32:00-07:00
Subject: RE: New vendor onboarding + early-pay ask

Looping in finance POV. Summary: we can support an early-pay pilot but need to lock down three items before engineering the PO routing: 1) signed supplemental agreement with the exact discount and invoice applicability; 2) vendor bank verification (micro-deposit or bank letter); 3) a single PO attribute ("early_pay=true") so invoices tagged by the vendor/PO hit our alternate AP queue.

On the numbers: 2%/10 is achievable but high for a pilot at scale. I'd propose Redwood offer 1.25%/10 for the first two invoices as a one-off pilot; we can evaluate results and escalate to 2% only with Exec approval. If Silverpine accepts 1.25%, we can auto-approve and have Ops enable the flag prior to the first invoice.

Grace — please prepare the vendor code and confirm whether the PO gateway accepts a custom JSON attribute in the PO webhook; if not, we can rely on PO number suffixing (e.g., SP-0001-EP) as a fallback.

Ben Carter
Director, Finance Operations
ben.carter@redwood.com

Date: 2027-05-27T15:03:00-07:00

1) Vendor record: I'll create the Silverpine vendor code (SP-4721) after we receive bank verification. Please confirm when you're ready for micro-deposits or upload a bank verification letter to the Drive folder (link below).
2) PO routing: our gateway supports a custom PO attribute; Ops will set "early_pay": true on the PO if Ben confirms the 1.25% pilot. If Silverpine accepts, we'll include guidance on the PO template so your billing system attaches the attribute.
3) Agreement and DocuSign: I'll upload a supplemental early-pay addendum and the PO to DocuSign. Lena — please review and sign; once DocuSign completes, Ben will authorize the PO flag.
4) Timeline: vendor record + verification by EOD Thursday, DocuSign signed by Friday, first PO issued next Monday.

title: Peering attestation — EU/US data paths and private VPC evidence
preview:
From: Sara Wilcox <sara@medbridgehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: sec-ops@medbridgehealth.com
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Request: EU/US data paths + private VPC peering evidence

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week. As part of our internal review (SOC 2 refresh + SIG submission), the security team needs a short package confirming the following before we can move into procurement: 

- Authoritative confirmation of regions used to serve EU customer data (are compute and any transient storage pinned to EU datacenters?), and an explanation of any cross-border egress paths.
- A VPC peering / PrivateLink design showing how MedBridge traffic would traverse to Redwood Dedicated / Private instances (diagram + subnets + NAT/egress points).
- Evidence that peering/privatelink traffic does not cross third-party transit providers outside the selected region (or, if it does, controls that mitigate exposure).
- CAIQ / SIG mapping items that reference the controls above (network segregation, peering controls, egress filtering).

If you can provide the diagram and a short attestation statement we can attach to the SIG, that would speed our procurement. Also, let us know if you can provide a signed attestation we can include in DocuSign.

Timeline: ideally by Wednesday EOD (US/PST).

Thanks,
Sara
Sara Wilcox
Head of Security | MedBridge Health

--
attachment: request_template.docx (stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sara Wilcox <sara@medbridgehealth.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 12:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: EU/US data paths + private VPC peering evidence

Hi Sara — thanks for the clear list. Looping in Irene (Security) and Marissa (Solutions) to assemble the artifacts.

Quick answers up front:
- We can commit to EU-only compute and short-lived KV/prefix caches for pinned EU deployments. Persistent artifacts (logs/metrics snapshots) are retained in-region when customers choose the EU hosting option; we’ll detail retention and audit timestamps in the packet.
- We have two private connectivity options: VPC peering (for Dedicated) or PrivateLink-style endpoints for Private deployments. Both support single-region peering and explicit egress NAT at customer-owned peering endpoints.
- We will provide a network diagram (redwood_vpc_peering_diagram_v1.pdf), a short runbook describing traffic paths (peering_runbook_privatelink_v2.md), and a CAIQ mapping spreadsheet that highlights controls relevant to peering and egress (caiaq_map_medbridge.xlsx).

Irene — can you confirm whether the peering path for EU-dedicated customers ever traverses our US backbone for control-plane synchronization? That’s the point Sara called out about transient cross-border egress.

Sara — I’ll draft a one-paragraph attestation for signature once Irene confirms the control-plane behavior.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference

--
attachment: redwood_vpc_peering_diagram_v1.pdf (diagram stub)

From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Sara Wilcox <sara@medbridgehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 09:34:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: EU/US data paths + private VPC peering evidence

Ben / Marissa / Sara — technical detail below. Quoting Ben's summary for context.

On Mon, May 24, 2027 at 12:18 PM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> - We can commit to EU-only compute and short-lived KV/prefix caches for pinned EU deployments.
> - Two private connectivity options: VPC peering (Dedicated) or PrivateLink for Private deployments.
>
Correct — addendum and specifics: 

1) Control-plane meta-sync:
   - Our control plane is a replicated service that sends non-customer payload metadata between control-plane regions for orchestration (not user prompt/response data). This replication happens over encrypted channels; the control plane does not contain prompt text or model outputs.

2) Data plane (customer data/processed tokens):
   - For customers who select EU region hosting, inference compute, KV caches, model weights, and ephemeral token buffers remain in the EU region and are never written to persistent US storage.
   - When using Dedicated VPC peering, customer's traffic is routed to the EU compute pool via a peered NIC; egress is controlled at your peering NAT.

3) Transit / third-party exposure:
   - Peering between customer VPC and Redwood VPC is single-hop over the cloud provider intra-region fabric (no third-party transit by default). If cross-region fallback occurs (e.g., failover to a warm standby in another region), we have routing policies that prefer in-region fallback and log any cross-region flows.

4) Evidence we will include:
   - redwood_vpc_peering_diagram_v1.pdf — annotated with subnets, NAT, and peering CIDR blocks.
   - peering_runbook_privatelink_v2.md — step-by-step peering setup, allowed ports, and egress policies.
   - caiaq_map_medbridge.xlsx — row-level mapping to SIG/CAIQ controls for network segregation and egress controls.
   - traceroute/flow-sample: small anonymized sample demonstrating in-region path (attached excerpt).

5) Suggested attestation language (draft):
   "Redwood Inference confirms that for customers who select EU-only hosting, the inference data plane (requests, ephemeral token buffers, and KV caches) is processed and retained within the selected EU region. Persistent logs and artifacts associated with EU deployments are retained in-region; control-plane orchestration metadata may be replicated across regions but does not contain customer prompt or response payloads."

Action items:
- Marissa: please package the CAIQ rows Sara requested and add an index that references the diagram pages.
- Ben: confirm whether MedBridge requires DocuSign-ready language or if a signed PDF attestation is sufficient.

Irene
Irene Choi
Director, Security Engineering — Redwood Inference

--
attachment: peering_runbook_privatelink_v2.md (stub)
attachment: traceroute_sample_eu_excerpt.txt (stub)
attachment: caiaq_map_medbridge.xlsx (stub)

From: Sara Wilcox <sara@medbridgehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: sec-ops@medbridgehealth.com, Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 26 May 2027 16:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: EU/US data paths + private VPC peering evidence

Ben / Irene / Marissa — this is very helpful, thank you. A few follow-ups: 

- The draft attestation language looks good; MedBridge legal requires DocuSign for all vendor attestations. Can you produce the same paragraph on Redwood letterhead and send a DocuSign link?

- One additional ask: do you have a short SLA appendix we can attach that lists expected failover behavior and whether cross-region fallback will ever expose payloads outside the EU for more than X minutes? Even a conservative number (e.g., < 15 minutes) will help our risk committee.

Hi Sara — thanks for the clear list. Looping in Irene (Security) and Marissa (Solutions) to assemble the artifacts.

Quick answers up front:
- We can commit to EU-only compute and short-lived KV/prefix caches for pinned EU deployments. Persistent artifacts (logs/metrics snapshots) are retained in-region when customers choose the EU hosting option; we’ll detail retention and audit timestamps in the packet.
- We have two private connectivity options: VPC peering (for Dedicated) or PrivateLink-style endpoints for Private deployments. Both support single-region peering and explicit egress NAT at customer-owned peering endpoints.
- We will provide a network diagram (redwood_vpc_peering_diagram_v1.pdf), a short runbook describing traffic paths (peering_runbook_privatelink_v2.md), and a CAIQ mapping spreadsheet that highlights controls relevant to peering and egress (caiaq_map_medbridge.xlsx).

Irene — can you confirm whether the peering path for EU-dedicated customers ever traverses our US backbone for control-plane synchronization? That’s the point Sara called out about transient cross-border egress.

Sara — I’ll draft a one-paragraph attestation for signature once Irene confirms the control-plane behavior.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference

From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Sara Wilcox <sara@medbridgehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 09:34:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: EU/US data paths + private VPC peering evidence

Ben / Marissa / Sara — technical detail below. Quoting Ben's summary for context.

On Mon, May 24, 2027 at 12:18 PM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> - We can commit to EU-only compute and short-lived KV/prefix caches for pinned EU deployments.
> - Two private connectivity options: VPC peering (Dedicated) or PrivateLink for Private deployments.
>
Correct — addendum and specifics: 

1) Control-plane meta-sync:
   - Our control plane is a replicated service that sends non-customer payload metadata between control-plane regions for orchestration (not user prompt/response data). This replication happens over encrypted channels; the control plane does not contain prompt text or model outputs.

2) Data plane (customer data/processed tokens):
   - For customers who select EU region hosting, inference compute, KV caches, model weights, and ephemeral token buffers remain in the EU region and are never written to persistent US storage.
   - When using Dedicated VPC peering, customer's traffic is routed to the EU compute pool via a peered NIC; egress is controlled at your peering NAT.

3) Transit / third-party exposure:
   - Peering between customer VPC and Redwood VPC is single-hop over the cloud provider intra-region fabric (no third-party transit by default). If cross-region fallback occurs (e.g., failover to a warm standby in another region), we have routing policies that prefer in-region fallback and log any cross-region flows.

4) Evidence we will include:
   - redwood_vpc_peering_diagram_v1.pdf — annotated with subnets, NAT, and peering CIDR blocks.
   - peering_runbook_privatelink_v2.md — step-by-step peering setup, allowed ports, and egress policies.
   - caiaq_map_medbridge.xlsx — row-level mapping to SIG/CAIQ controls for network segregation and egress controls.
   - traceroute/flow-sample: small anonymized sample demonstrating in-region path (attached excerpt).

5) Suggested attestation language (draft):
   "Redwood Inference confirms that for customers who select EU-only hosting, the inference data plane (requests, ephemeral token buffers, and KV caches) is processed and retained within the selected EU region. Persistent logs and artifacts associated with EU deployments are retained in-region; control-plane orchestration metadata may be replicated across regions but does not contain customer prompt or response payloads."

Action items:
- Marissa: please package the CAIQ rows Sara requested and add an index that references the diagram pages.
- Ben: confirm whether MedBridge requires DocuSign-ready language or if a signed PDF attestation is sufficient.

Irene

title: SparkLane throughput exception — orchestration and customer update
preview:
From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Spike in p95 and throughput drop observed for SparkLane

Team —

We're seeing a sustained p95 latency spike (~2.6s from baseline 0.7s) and a ~38% drop in tokens/sec for SparkLane (account DL-2027-0098) starting at 08:48 PDT. Impacted routes: /v1/generate (model-v1.3 and v1.4 variants), highest error concentration on region-us-west-2.

Quick facts:
- Affected customers: SparkLane (production), observed during their daily batch ingest.
- Metrics attached: slo_metrics.png (p95, p99, throughput traces).
- Raw traces/logs collected and attached: sparklane_logs.zip.

What we've tried so far (automated):
- Autoscaler kicked; some capacity added but throttling persisted.
- Prefix cache is being hit less than expected (cache hit ratio dropped from 0.82 -> 0.33).

Immediate mitigations we recommend executing now:
1) Reduce batch size for SparkLane route to 1 for the next 30 minutes.
2) Roll traffic to model-v1.2 (previously stable) as a temporary fallback.
3) Open short-term throttling rule to limit burst traffic from their API keys to protect multi-tenant queues.

Asks:
- Ben/Marissa: please prepare a short customer note (ack + ETA + ask to pause large batch jobs).
- Karthik/Connor: can you run the fallback to v1.2 and confirm the KV cache behavior?
- I'll continue digging into kernel scheduling and cache eviction logs.

Attaching runbook_v2.pdf with suggested steps for rollback and quick triage.

— Evelyn
Evelyn Hart
SRE — Redwood Inference



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Lauren Park <lauren.park@sparklane.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 09:23:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Spike in p95 and throughput drop observed for SparkLane

Hi Lauren —

Thanks for flagging this, and sorry for the disruption this morning. We're actively investigating; immediate actions we've proposed to your engineering team:
- Short-term fallback to model-v1.2 (expected to restore typical p95).
- Temporarily reduce batch sizes for scheduled ingest jobs to avoid burst pressure.
- We'll apply a burst throttle to keep other customers unaffected.

We're targeting an initial mitigation update by 11:00 PDT and a follow-up within 3 hours to confirm stabilization. Can you hold off on running your large daily ingest at 10:00 for now? That will help us validate the fallback.

If you'd like, we can open a quick conference at 11:00 to walk through actions — I'll send an invite if that works.

Apologies again — we're on it.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0123



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 10:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Spike in p95 and throughput drop observed for SparkLane

On it — quick technical log:

Actions taken (10:02–10:08 PDT):
- Rotated SparkLane traffic to model-v1.2 (gradual 50% -> 100% over 4 minutes).
- Reduced batching for their API key to batch_size=1 for critical routes.
- Forced an eviction window on KV caches for the route to clear possible stale partitions.

Observations:
- After fallback to v1.2 p95 improved to ~0.9s within ~3 minutes (still > baseline but acceptable).
- Throughput recovered to ~88% of baseline; residual variance appears tied to worker-side scheduling (kernel queueing) under multi-tenant load.
- There are a handful of long-tail requests with large context lengths that are spiking CPU/GPU time; I flagged a set in sparklane_logs.zip under /slow-reqs/ for triage.

Next steps:
- I'll keep the fallback in place for the next 6 hours while we analyze traces. Recommend teams consider a short capacity bump (1 node) in us-west-2 to reduce queueing risk if traffic normalizes.
- Assigning Connor to check kernel scheduling knobs and batching heuristics tonight.

PagerDuty incident: PD-987654
Runbook reference: runbook_v2.pdf

— Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Staff Engineer, Serve Runtime



From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Lauren Park <lauren.park@sparklane.com>, SparkLane Eng <eng@sparklane.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 25 May 2027 11:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Update: mitigation in place, request to pause heavy ingest jobs

Hi Lauren / SparkLane Eng —

On Tue, May 25, 2027 at 9:23 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Hi Lauren —
> 
> Thanks for flagging this, and sorry for the disruption this morning. We're actively investigating; immediate actions we've proposed to your engineering team: ...

Quick update from our side: after switching traffic to model-v1.2 and reducing batch sizes we see meaningful improvement: p95 ~0.9s and throughput ~88% baseline. Karthik's team is continuing to analyze traces for root cause (likely interaction of long-context requests + scheduler preemption).

Request: Please delay your scheduled heavy ingest at 10:00 PDT until we confirm a full recovery (we expect a follow-up by 14:00 PDT). Delaying that job will reduce the likelihood of regression while we complete traces and consider a temporary capacity add.

If you'd like a quick handoff call, schedule here: https://calendly.com/redwood/sparklane-incident-30m

We'll send a final status update once we've validated.

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Lauren Park <lauren.park@sparklane.com>, SparkLane Eng <eng@sparklane.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 26 May 2027 14:30:00 -07:00
Subject: SparkLane incident 2027-05-25 — resolution and next steps

Hi Lauren / Team —

Final update and summary:
- Incident window: 2027-05-25 08:48 — 2027-05-26 13:15 PDT (mitigations applied 25 May 10:02 PDT).
- Primary impact: p95 latency spike and ~38% throughput drop for some gen routes during SparkLane's heavy ingest.
- Mitigations executed: fallback to model-v1.2, reduced batching, short-term burst throttle, KV cache eviction.
- Immediate outcome: p95 returned to ~0.75–0.95s, throughput near baseline after capacity stabilization.

Root cause (preliminary): Interaction between very long-context requests in a subset of SparkLane jobs and kernel scheduling under peak multi-tenant load; this led to queueing and cache fragmentation. Engineering is preparing a targeted fix (scheduler tuning + defensive request-size limits) and a postmortem.

We've scheduled a 45-minute postmortem for Thursday 2027-05-28 10:00 PDT to walk through the detailed timeline, corrective actions, and long-term mitigations. Invite & postmortem doc: https://drive.redwood/company/sparklane-incident-20270525

As a gesture for the disruption, Redwood will apply a one-time credit of 10% for May usage to the account; your billing lead and I will coordinate details offline.

If you'd like a deeper technical readout, Connor will send the trace summary and slow-req list to your eng team later today.

Thanks for your patience — and we appreciate the collaboration on this.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0123

title: Mutual Action: proposed load window & sizing for traffic replay
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@acme.io>, Daniel Ortega <daniel.ortega@acme.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 23 May 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Mutual Action: proposed load window & sizing for traffic replay

Hi Samantha / Daniel —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps to lock the MAP milestone for the load/perf replay:

1) Goal & success criteria
- Execute a traffic replay covering expected peak QPS and bursts with end-to-end observability (latency P50/P95, error rate)
- Validate dedicated capacity sizing that meets target SLOs during peak windows

2) Proposed test windows (pick one):
- Option A: Jun 14–16 (preferred for Redwood ops)
- Option B: Jun 21–23 (preferred if you need extra prep time)

3) Preliminary asks from Acme (customer)
- Provide traffic snapshot / qps profile (CSV) for a typical peak day + 7-day rolling baseline
- Confirm any PII/data masking requirements for replayed payloads
- Ownership: Samantha to deliver the traffic snapshot and procurement timeline

4) Redwood owners & actions
- Ben (me): schedule the run window and coordinate ops on our side; block the slot tentatively until you confirm
- Vivek (solutions): sizing recommendation for dedicated pool and fallback options (hosted burst)
- Marissa (CS): finalize the replay runbook and coordinate on-call coverage

5) Dependencies & timeline
- We need access to telemetry/metrics export and a stable snapshot at least 10 business days before the run to generate the replay artifacts
- Procurement: if you need dedicated capacity reserved, lead time is ~4–6 weeks after contract signature

I attached a draft MAP spreadsheet with milestone rows (acme_map_draft_v2.xlsx) and a short checklist. Please confirm which test window works and share the traffic profile when you can. If you prefer a quick call to align, I can set one up tomorrow.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise AE | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1-415-555-0123

Attachments: acme_map_draft_v2.xlsx

To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 09:05:00 -0700

Ben —

Appreciate the clear recap. We ran a quick pull of our traffic and attached a short profile (traffic_profile_summary.csv). High level: peak QPS on our storefront is ~12k sustained during promo peaks with short 95th-percentile bursts up to ~25k for ~3–7 minutes. Average seq length is ~160 tokens, with checkout flows spiking longer.

Our procurement runway is tighter than we hoped — if we need dedicated reserved capacity the earliest we could sign is mid-June (about 4–6 weeks). If Redwood can run an initial validation in a hosted/burst model earlier that would help unblock us.

We can target the Jun 21–23 window if that aligns with your ops availability. I’ll also confirm any PII exclusions and deliver the full 7-day baseline by EOD Wed 5/26.

—Samantha

To: Erin Shaw <erin.shaw@stellarhealth.com>

Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Next steps: vendor enrollment & tax docs after demo with StellarHealth

Hi Erin —

Thanks again for taking the time today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so procurement has everything they need to evaluate Redwood for production: 

1) Requested docs: W-9 (for US entity) or W-8BEN-E (if relevant) + completed vendor packet (banking info + ACH instructions).
2) Payment preference: you mentioned PO-first for initial pilot; please confirm whether Procurement wants invoices to 30 net and if they require a PO number to be present on invoices/contract.
3) Marketplace: you asked whether we can be transacted via the Cloud Marketplace (GCP/AWS). We support marketplace billing but it does add time for vendor enrollment — it can be a parallel path.
4) Vendor portal: if Procurement prefers a portal onboarding flow, we can send a vendor packet PDF and an onboarding link to complete tax and banking details.

I attached our W-9 and a short vendor packet with instructions. Can you confirm: PO vs card (which would speed pilot), and whether Stellar prefers a vendor portal enrollment or email-based paper process?

Proposed internal owners: Marcus (finance) will handle banking and tax details; Neha (onboarding) will manage portal/invite and the vendor packet.

If helpful, happy to jump on a quick 20-min call with Procurement later this week.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | (415) 555-0192

Attachments: Redwood_W9_2027.pdf (application/pdf), vendor_packet_instructions_Stellar.pdf (application/pdf)

To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@stellarhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 17:30:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Next steps: vendor enrollment & tax docs after demo with StellarHealth

Thanks Ben — great demo this afternoon.

Short answers:
- We will almost certainly need a PO for the pilot (internal policy), so Procurement wants the PO route. Net30 is our standard payment term but I will confirm if pilot exceptions are allowed.
- Please send the vendor packet and W-9; I will need a completed packet back with banking details and a point of contact in case of questions.
- Marketplace would be nice, but procurement prioritizes getting the vendor set up and the PO issued first. We can revisit marketplace later.

Can you confirm whether Redwood requires a signed MSA before PO issuance or if we can do a PO with terms referenced and finalize the MSA in parallel? Also include a contact for Treasury so I can push the packet through.

Best,
Erin Shaw
Senior Procurement Manager | StellarHealth
erin.shaw@stellarhealth.com

Team — looping you in on StellarHealth's ask. They prefer PO-first for the pilot. Questions from Erin: 1) MSA timing vs PO issuance, 2) Treasury contact for pushing packet.

Marcus — can you confirm what tax/banking docs we need to return to Procurement to avoid delays? If we provide W-9 + vendor packet with ACH form, is that sufficient for a PO to be issued on their side?

Neha — please prepare the vendor portal invite and attach the vendor packet PDF. If Stellar cannot complete the portal quickly, we'll accept emailed packet scans. Also, please generate a one-page list of expected timeline steps for Procurement (e.g., packet complete -> vendor ID -> PO issuance -> kickoff) that I can share with Erin.

I attached the W-9 again in case they missed it.

Thanks — Ben
